review_summary: Textual grounding is sufficient for concept-focused questions, and no separate video clip is needed.

question_bank.question: Why is ordinary next-word prediction not a suitable pretraining objective for a bidirectional encoder?
choices: The encoder can attend to both sides, so the target token can leak through the input context.
The encoder has no token embeddings to update.
The encoder can only process one token at a time from left to right.
Next-word prediction requires labeled sentiment examples.
question_bank.explanation: A bidirectional encoder sees information from both directions. If asked to predict a token that remains visible in its context, the task can become trivial instead of teaching robust representations.

question_bank.question: What does masked language modeling train an encoder to do?
choices: Reconstruct selected missing tokens using the surrounding bidirectional context.
Generate text strictly left to right without seeing future tokens.
Translate every input sentence into another language.
Predict only whether two documents have the same author.
question_bank.explanation: Masked language modeling corrupts some input positions and computes loss on those positions. The encoder must use both left and right context to infer the missing words.

question_bank.question: Why did BERT sometimes leave selected tokens unchanged or replace them randomly instead of always using a mask token?
choices: To make the model build useful contextual representations even when explicit mask tokens are absent at test time.
To prevent the model from learning subword embeddings.
To make every sentence exactly the same length.
To convert the encoder into a left-to-right decoder.
question_bank.explanation: If training only used artificial mask symbols, the model could rely too much on a pattern that does not appear in ordinary downstream inputs. Random and unchanged cases make training closer to real use.

question_bank.question: What was the next-sentence prediction task in BERT-style pretraining?
choices: Given two text segments, predict whether the second is the true continuation of the first.
Given a prefix, predict only the next subword token.
Given a sentence, remove all punctuation and predict it back.
Given two words, predict whether they have the same spelling.
question_bank.explanation: Next-sentence prediction adds a sentence-level objective: the model must judge whether one segment naturally follows another, using segment information along with token representations.

question_bank.question: What was a major practical effect of BERT-style encoder pretraining?
choices: A single pretrained encoder could be adapted to many tasks by adding task-specific heads and fine-tuning.
It eliminated the need for any downstream data.
It made hand-written linguistic rules mandatory for every task.
It replaced all tokenization with character-by-character decoding only.
question_bank.explanation: A pretrained bidirectional encoder provides general contextual representations. Task-specific heads can then map those representations to outputs for classification, QA, or other tasks.
